results.heading: 2.1 LLMs and token probabilities *(Theory)*
results.label: human
results.reasoning:
AI INDICATORS:
- "The way LLMs work is, at every generation step they compute a probability distribution over their entire vocabulary, sample a token from that distribution, and move on (a token, here, being a sub-word piece — sometimes a whole word, sometimes a fragment of one)." — descriptive, explanatory passages that detail processes, mechanisms, or relationships (e.g., scientific principles, physical descriptions, or technical explanations).
- "The log-probability of whichever token got sampled is basically a free measurement of how "expected" that token was given the context up to that point, and that measurement turns out to sit at the centre of pretty much every detection method that touches log-probabilities at all." — descriptive, explanatory passages that detail processes, mechanisms, or relationships (e.g., scientific principles, physical descriptions, or technical explanations).
- "DetectGPT reads the generator's log-probabilities — the idea being that if you assume some specific LLM wrote the candidate text, you can score how likely that text was under that model, and machine text tends to occupy regions of high probability under its source LLM." — descriptive, explanatory passages that detail processes, mechanisms, or relationships (e.g., scientific principles, physical descriptions, or technical explanations).

HUMAN INDICATORS:
- "The single-token classification trick is the natural thing to try once you've stared at this picture long enough: ask the classifier a yes/no question, generate exactly one token, and read off which of the two affirmative-or-negative answers got the higher log-probability." — conversational interjections, or self-corrections, such as "EDIT," or phrases that mimic spoken thought patterns.
- "The difference between those two log-probabilities is a continuous decision margin you didn't have to spend extra inference time to extract, which matters when you're running this at the scale of even a small thesis dataset." — conversational

results.heading: 3.2 Two-phase classifier *(D1, D2, D3)*
results.label: AI
results.reasoning:
AI INDICATORS:
- "The pipeline starts with policy induction and ends with a small log-probability calibration layer, and the two steps are doing almost nothing in common with each other." — "Pay special attention to descriptive, explanatory passages that detail processes, mechanisms, or relationships (e.g., scientific principles, physical descriptions, or technical explanations)."
- "We score the candidate against F0.5 on a held-out training subset (a hundred examples is enough, five hundred is plenty), and when it gets things wrong we feed those examples back and ask for a revised version; revisions only stick if F0.5 actually moves up, and the loop terminates either when improvements plateau (operationally, when the moving Δ stays under 0.005 across the last three accepted edits) or at the thirty-iteration budget, whichever shows up first." — "Pay special attention to descriptive, explanatory passages that detail processes, mechanisms, or relationships (e.g., scientific principles, physical descriptions, or technical explanations)."
- "On those features we fit a small calibration model — either a single threshold on logp(yes) − logp(no) or a logistic regression over the three log-probabilities — on the validation split to maximise F0.5, and that fitted layer plus the frozen policy is what we ultimately report performance for on the held-out test split." — "Pay special attention to descriptive, explanatory passages that detail processes, mechanisms, or relationships (e.g., scientific principles, physical descriptions, or technical explanations)."
